quiz_questions.question_text: Which of the following best defines "Growth rate comparison"?

options.text: Speeding up: v(t) and a(t) have the same sign; slowing down: they have opposite signs
A point where concavity changes; f''(x) = 0 or undefined AND the sign of f''(x) changes
As x→∞: ln(x) << xⁿ << eˣ << x!; each function eventually dominates those to its left
An interior point c where f'(c) = 0 (horizontal tangent) or f'(c) does not exist (corner, cusp, or vertical tangent)

quiz_questions.explanation: "Growth rate comparison" is defined as: As x→∞: ln(x) << xⁿ << eˣ << x!; each function eventually dominates those to its left. The other options describe different concepts.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which of the following best defines "L'Hôpital's Rule"?

options.text: If lim f(x)/g(x) gives 0/0 or ∞/∞, then lim f(x)/g(x) = lim f'(x)/g'(x), provided the latter exists
Vertical: where denominator = 0 (numerator ≠ 0); horizontal: limits at ±∞; oblique: when degree(num) = degree(den) + 1
Priority for choosing u: Logarithmic > Inverse trig > Algebraic > Trigonometric > Exponential; u is differentiated, dv is integrated
Derived from the limit definition using sin(x+h) = sin x cos h + cos x sin h and special limits

quiz_questions.explanation: "L'Hôpital's Rule" is defined as: If lim f(x)/g(x) gives 0/0 or ∞/∞, then lim f(x)/g(x) = lim f'(x)/g'(x), provided the latter exists. The other options describe different concepts.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which of the following best defines "Indeterminate forms"?

options.text: d/dx[f(x)/g(x)] = [f'(x)g(x) - f(x)g'(x)] / [g(x)]²; 'low d-high minus high d-low over low squared'
P(t) = K/(1 + Ae^(-rt)) where A = (K-P₀)/P₀; produces an S-shaped (sigmoidal) curve
0/0, ∞/∞, 0·∞, ∞-∞, 0⁰, 1^∞, ∞⁰; L'Hôpital's directly handles the first two
μ = E[X] = ∫₋∞^∞ x·f(x)dx; the 'center of mass' of the probability distribution

quiz_questions.explanation: "Indeterminate forms" is defined as: 0/0, ∞/∞, 0·∞, ∞-∞, 0⁰, 1^∞, ∞⁰; L'Hôpital's directly handles the first two. The other options describe different concepts.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which of the following best defines "Dominance"?

options.text: Speeding up: v(t) and a(t) have the same sign; slowing down: they have opposite signs
Function f dominates g if lim(x→∞) f(x)/g(x) = ∞; equivalently, lim(x→∞) g(x)/f(x) = 0
L(x) = f(a) + f'(a)(x - a); the tangent line at x = a used to estimate f(x) for x near a
At a critical point c: f' changes + to -: local max; f' changes - to +: local min; no sign change: neither

quiz_questions.explanation: "Dominance" is defined as: Function f dominates g if lim(x→∞) f(x)/g(x) = ∞; equivalently, lim(x→∞) g(x)/f(x) = 0. The other options describe different concepts.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which of the following best defines "Asymptotic equivalence"?

options.text: Two functions are asymptotically equivalent if lim(x→∞) f(x)/g(x) = 1
lim[f ± g] = lim f ± lim g; lim[f·g] = lim f · lim g; lim[f/g] = lim f / lim g (if lim g ≠ 0); lim[fⁿ] = (lim f)ⁿ
Product rule: two functions multiplied; Quotient rule: division; Chain rule: composition (function inside another function)
A grid of short line segments showing the slope dy/dx = f(x,y) at each point; visual representation of a DE

quiz_questions.explanation: "Asymptotic equivalence" is defined as: Two functions are asymptotically equivalent if lim(x→∞) f(x)/g(x) = 1. The other options describe different concepts.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which term is described by: "As x→∞: ln(x) << xⁿ << eˣ << x!; each function eventually dominates those to its left"?

options.text: Position, velocity, acceleration
Growth rate comparison
Restricting domain
Integration by parts formula

quiz_questions.explanation: This description refers to "Growth rate comparison".

quiz_questions.question_text: Which term is described by: "If lim f(x)/g(x) gives 0/0 or ∞/∞, then lim f(x)/g(x) = lim f'(x)/g'(x), provided the latter exists"?

options.text: L'Hôpital's Rule
Limit laws
Infinite limit
Lorenz curve

quiz_questions.explanation: This description refers to "L'Hôpital's Rule".

quiz_questions.question_text: Which term is described by: "0/0, ∞/∞, 0·∞, ∞-∞, 0⁰, 1^∞, ∞⁰; L'Hôpital's directly handles the first two"?

options.text: Fractional exponent derivatives
Hypothesis checking
Implicit differentiation with time
Indeterminate forms

quiz_questions.explanation: This description refers to "Indeterminate forms".

quiz_questions.question_text: Which term is described by: "Function f dominates g if lim(x→∞) f(x)/g(x) = ∞; equivalently, lim(x→∞) g(x)/f(x) = 0"?

options.text: Dominance
Newton's method
Second derivative test
Integration by parts indicators

quiz_questions.explanation: This description refers to "Dominance".

quiz_questions.question_text: Which term is described by: "Two functions are asymptotically equivalent if lim(x→∞) f(x)/g(x) = 1"?

options.text: One-sided limits
Asymptotic equivalence
Left/Right/Midpoint sum
Second derivative test

quiz_questions.explanation: This description refers to "Asymptotic equivalence".

quiz_questions.question_text: Which of the following statements about Comparing Limit Behaviors is accurate?

options.text: Comparing Limit Behaviors involves analyzing how different function types grow relative to each other.
Success requires clear justification of setup, correct antiderivatives, appropriate use of technology, and precise interpretation of results in the given context.
Choose u and dv from the integrand using the LIATE priority: Logarithmic, Inverse trig, Algebraic, Trigonometric, Exponential (choose u from earlier in the list).
Implicit Differentiation finds dy/dx when y is not explicitly solved as a function of x.

quiz_questions.explanation: The correct statement describes Comparing Limit Behaviors as covered in this lesson.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which statement correctly describes an aspect of Comparing Limit Behaviors?

options.text: Trigonometric substitutions handle expressions with √(a²-x²), √(a²+x²), or √(x²-a²).
Antiderivatives and Indefinite Integrals introduce integration as the reverse of differentiation.
These formulas arise from the Pythagorean theorem applied to infinitesimal arc segments: ds = √(dx² + dy²) = √(1 + (dy/dx)²)dx.
Exponential functions (eˣ) eventually dominate polynomial functions (xⁿ), which dominate logarithmic functions (ln x) as x → ∞.

quiz_questions.explanation: The correct statement describes Comparing Limit Behaviors as covered in this lesson.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which is true specifically about Comparing Limit Behaviors?

options.text: Average Value of a Function extends the concept of arithmetic mean to continuous functions.
Energy conservation connects work to kinetic and potential energy changes.
Antiderivatives: Integration Strategies develops a systematic approach to choosing the right technique.
L'Hôpital's Rule resolves indeterminate forms 0/0 or ∞/∞ by comparing derivatives: lim f(x)/g(x) = lim f'(x)/g'(x).

quiz_questions.explanation: The correct statement describes Comparing Limit Behaviors as covered in this lesson.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which best explains a key idea of Comparing Limit Behaviors?

options.text: These combined techniques are essential for related rates problems and advanced curve analysis.
These derivatives are essential for modeling growth, decay, and natural phenomena.
Limits and Continuity are the foundation of calculus.
Understanding growth rates is essential for comparing algorithms, modeling populations, and analyzing asymptotic behavior.

quiz_questions.explanation: The correct statement describes Comparing Limit Behaviors as covered in this lesson.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which accurately reflects the content on Comparing Limit Behaviors?

options.text: These are important for integration and solving applied problems.
These combined techniques are essential for related rates problems and advanced curve analysis.
Comparing Limit Behaviors involves analyzing how different function types grow relative to each other.
This distinction is crucial: a car's speedometer shows instantaneous velocity, not average velocity.

quiz_questions.explanation: The correct statement describes Comparing Limit Behaviors as covered in this lesson.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which of the following is a real-world application of Comparing Limit Behaviors?

options.text: Marathon pace strategy: analyzing a runner's velocity function to identify periods of acceleration (increasing velocity) and deceleration (decreasing velocity)
Rocket propulsion: as fuel is burned, the rocket becomes lighter, changing the force equation—integration handles the continuously varying mass
Algorithm analysis in computer science: O(n log n) sorting algorithms (merge sort) outperform O(n²) algorithms (bubble sort) for large datasets
Estimating total rainfall from rate-of-rainfall data: ∫(rainfall rate)dt gives total accumulated precipitation over a storm event

quiz_questions.explanation: This is a real-world application of Comparing Limit Behaviors.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which example demonstrates the practical relevance of Comparing Limit Behaviors?

options.text: Bacterial growth: exponential growth eventually outpaces any polynomial model, explaining why unchecked bacterial populations can become dangerously large in hours
The Squeeze Theorem proving that oscillating functions with decreasing amplitude converge: lim(x→∞) sin(x)/x = 0 since -1/x ≤ sin(x)/x ≤ 1/x
Control systems engineering: inverse tangent functions model phase shifts in frequency response, with derivatives indicating sensitivity to frequency changes
Planetary orbits: elliptical orbits x²/a² + y²/b² = 1 analyzed with implicit differentiation to find orbital velocity direction at each point

quiz_questions.explanation: This is a real-world application of Comparing Limit Behaviors.

quiz_questions.question_text: How is Comparing Limit Behaviors applied in real-world contexts?

options.text: Population growth modeling: d/dt[P(t)] where P involves power functions helps ecologists predict changes in wildlife populations
Moore's Law: the roughly exponential growth of transistor density in microchips (doubling every ~2 years) eventually creating unforeseen limits in physics
Marathon pace strategy: analyzing a runner's velocity function to identify periods of acceleration (increasing velocity) and deceleration (decreasing velocity)
Traffic analysis: given a graph of vehicle entry rate, computing total vehicles and identifying peak congestion periods

quiz_questions.explanation: This is a real-world application of Comparing Limit Behaviors.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which scenario illustrates the principles of Comparing Limit Behaviors?

options.text: Average voltage of AC power: V_avg = (1/T)∫₀ᵀ V(t)dt; for sinusoidal AC, the average over a full cycle is zero, motivating the use of RMS voltage
Financial analysis: if an investment grows from $1000 to $1200 over a year, MVT guarantees the instantaneous growth rate was exactly 20% at some moment
Algorithm analysis in computer science: O(n log n) sorting algorithms (merge sort) outperform O(n²) algorithms (bubble sort) for large datasets
Probability: the Cauchy distribution has CDF (1/π)arctan(x) + 1/2, obtained by integrating the probability density 1/(π(1+x²))

quiz_questions.explanation: This is a real-world application of Comparing Limit Behaviors.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which best shows Comparing Limit Behaviors in practice?

options.text: Water tank problems: given inflow rate and outflow rate functions, compute water level at any time t and find when the tank is fullest
The Squeeze Theorem proving that oscillating functions with decreasing amplitude converge: lim(x→∞) sin(x)/x = 0 since -1/x ≤ sin(x)/x ≤ 1/x
Coulomb's law: electric force F = kq₁q₂/r² → ∞ as distance r → 0, creating a vertical asymptote that reflects the impossibility of zero separation between charges
Bacterial growth: exponential growth eventually outpaces any polynomial model, explaining why unchecked bacterial populations can become dangerously large in hours

quiz_questions.explanation: This is a real-world application of Comparing Limit Behaviors.
